title: WebGPU for Browser-Based ML Inference
excerpt: Early experiments running a small transformer entirely client-side — what worked, what didn't, and why this changes everything.

title: The Demo-to-Production Gap in AI Products
excerpt: The gap between 'demo' and 'production' is where most AI startups die. It's not the model — it's the 10,000 edge cases around it.
content:
Every AI startup I've watched closely has the same arc: impressive demo, excited investors, then a long painful slog through production reality. The gap between *it works on my laptop* and *it works for a million users* is where most of them die.

## It's Never the Model

The model is the easy part. You can swap models, fine-tune them, prompt-engineer your way to decent outputs. What kills you is **everything around the model**.

### Input Validation

What happens when users send you garbage? Not malicious garbage — just the infinite variety of real-world input that your training data never covered. The misspellings, the mixed languages, the copy-pasted text with invisible Unicode characters.

### Output Safety

Your model will occasionally produce something unhinged. You need guardrails, but guardrails that are too tight kill the product. Finding that balance is an ongoing negotiation, not a one-time engineering decision.

### Latency Budgets

Users will tolerate **2 seconds** of loading for a "thinking" animation. They will **not** tolerate 8 seconds. Every optimization matters, and the difference between P50 and P99 latency can make or break your UX.

A typical latency budget breakdown:

```python
# Latency budget for AI-powered autocomplete
TOTAL_BUDGET_MS = 2000

context_retrieval = 200   # RAG lookup
model_inference = 1200    # LLM generation
post_processing = 100     # safety filters + formatting
network_overhead = 300    # round trip
headroom = 200            # buffer for P99 spikes

assert sum([
    context_retrieval, model_inference,
    post_processing, network_overhead, headroom
]) <= TOTAL_BUDGET_MS
```

### Cost Management

Running inference at scale is expensive. The math that works at 100 users breaks at 100,000. You need caching strategies, batching, model distillation — all the boring infrastructure work that demos skip.

---

The teams that survive are the ones that treat the model as **one component** in a larger system, not the system itself.

title: What TikTok Scale Teaches You About Systems
excerpt: When your 'edge case' affects 50 million users, there are no edge cases. Working on distributed systems at this scale changes how you think about everything.
content:
Working on distributed systems at TikTok scale fundamentally rewired how I approach engineering problems. When your platform serves billions of requests daily, every assumption you hold about software breaks in interesting ways.

## Edge Cases Don't Exist

The first thing you learn is that edge cases don't exist. If something *can* happen, it *will* happen — at scale, constantly. A one-in-a-million bug fires a thousand times a day. You stop thinking in terms of "unlikely" and start thinking in terms of **"when."**

## Consistency Is a Spectrum

The second revelation is about consistency. In a distributed system, "consistent" is a spectrum, not a boolean. You learn to ask not *"is this consistent?"* but *"how inconsistent can we tolerate?"*

| Consistency Level | Latency Cost | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Eventual (~500ms) | Low | Feed rankings, like counts |
| Causal | Medium | Comment threads, chat |
| Linearizable | High | Payments, inventory |

Sometimes eventual consistency with a 500ms window is fine. Sometimes you need linearizability and you pay the performance cost willingly.

## Network Partitions Are Real

Network partitions aren't theoretical. They happen during routine deployments, during traffic spikes, during datacenter maintenance. Your system needs to degrade gracefully, not catastrophically. The CAP theorem stops being an academic concept and becomes a daily design constraint.

## Monitoring Is Your Primary UI

You spend more time looking at dashboards and traces than at code. The ability to quickly diagnose why P99 latency spiked from 50ms to 200ms at 3 AM is a core skill, not a nice-to-have.

> Simple systems survive. Every clever optimization, every abstract layer, every "elegant" architecture adds cognitive load and failure surface. The systems that run reliably at scale are almost always **boring**. Boring is a feature.

title: How Rust's Ownership Model Rewired My Brain
excerpt: Even in TypeScript now, I instinctively think about who 'owns' a piece of data. Rust didn't just teach me a language — it changed how I reason about state.
content:
I started learning Rust about two years ago, mostly out of curiosity. What I didn't expect was how profoundly it would change the way I think about code in every other language.

Rust's ownership model forces you to be explicit about something most languages let you ignore: who owns a piece of data, and when does it stop existing? In JavaScript or Python, you create objects and let the garbage collector figure it out. In Rust, you decide.

This sounds restrictive, and at first it is. You fight the borrow checker. You restructure code that would be trivial in other languages. You wonder why you're doing this to yourself.

Then something clicks. You start seeing ownership patterns everywhere. In a React component, who 'owns' a piece of state? When a prop is passed down three levels, that's a borrow. When you clone data into a new component's local state, that's a move. When a useEffect cleanup function runs, that's a drop.

In TypeScript, I now instinctively ask: is this function taking ownership of this data, or borrowing it? Should this be a deep clone or a reference? When does this subscription need to be cleaned up?

The real gift of Rust isn't performance or memory safety — it's a mental framework for reasoning about data flow. Once you have it, you can't unsee it. Every language becomes clearer because you're thinking about the same fundamental questions: who has this data, what can they do with it, and when should it go away.

title: Open Source Is About Trust, Not Code
excerpt: GSoC taught me that open source isn't about code — it's about building trust with a community of strangers through consistent, transparent work.
content:
My Google Summer of Code experience fundamentally changed how I understand open source. Before GSoC, I thought contributing to open source was about writing good code and submitting pull requests. I was wrong.

Open source is a trust network. Code is just the artifact. What actually matters is the social infrastructure: how you communicate decisions, how you handle disagreements, how you show up consistently over time.

My first major PR was technically solid. It solved the problem, had tests, followed the style guide. It sat unreviewed for two weeks. I was frustrated until a senior maintainer explained: they didn't know me yet. They couldn't trust that my code did what I said it did without investing significant review time. I hadn't earned that trust.

So I started smaller. I reviewed other people's PRs. I answered questions in issues. I wrote documentation. I showed up in community calls. None of this was code, but all of it was contributing. Over weeks, I became a known quantity. My PRs got reviewed faster because maintainers had calibrated their trust in my judgment.

The lesson extends far beyond open source. Every engineering team runs on trust. When a senior engineer approves a PR with a quick glance, they're not being lazy — they've calibrated their trust in the author over months of consistent quality.

Building that trust is slow, deliberate work. There are no shortcuts. But once you have it, everything else — code reviews, architectural decisions, production incidents — gets dramatically easier.

title: AI Coding: Context Is Everything
excerpt: The core of AI-assisted coding isn't letting AI write code — it's letting AI understand your codebase context to make decisions that fit the architecture.
content:
The discourse around AI coding tools is stuck on the wrong question. Everyone asks 'can AI write code?' The answer is obviously yes. The real question is: can AI write code that fits?

Fitting means understanding context. Not just syntax, not just the function signature — the full context. Why does this module exist? What patterns does the team follow? What are the implicit constraints that nobody wrote down?

I've been using AI coding assistants daily for over a year. The difference between a useful suggestion and a useless one almost always comes down to context. When the AI understands the codebase — the abstractions, the naming conventions, the architectural boundaries — its suggestions feel like they came from a team member. When it doesn't, they feel like Stack Overflow answers pasted into the wrong project.

This is why RAG over codebases matters more than model size. A smaller model with perfect context will outperform a larger model working blind. The teams building the best AI coding tools understand this. They're not just throwing code at GPT-4 — they're building sophisticated context retrieval systems that give the model the right information at the right time.

The implication for developers is clear: if you want AI to help you effectively, invest in making your codebase legible. Clear module boundaries, consistent patterns, good naming. The same things that help human developers also help AI developers. The codebase is the prompt.
